title: EXPOSÉ VII COHOMOLOGY OF SOME CLASSICAL SCHEMES AND COHOMOLOGICAL THEORY OF CHERN CLASSES

pages.title: EXPOSÉ VII COHOMOLOGY OF SOME CLASSICAL SCHEMES AND COHOMOLOGICAL THEORY OF CHERN CLASSES

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VII
COHOMOLOGY OF SOME CLASSICAL SCHEMES
AND
COHOMOLOGICAL THEORY OF CHERN CLASSESby
J. P. JOUANOLOU

Throughout the text, we suppose given an integer \( u>0\) and we set \(A=\mathbb Z/ u\mathbb Z\); the residue characteristics of all the schemes considered are prime to the integer \( u\). If \(X\) is such a scheme, we denote by \(\mu_X\) the sheaf of \( u\)-th roots of unity and by \(A^*(X)\) the cohomology ring
\[\bigoplus_i H^{2i}(X,\mu_X^{\otimes i})\]
with the cup-product as multiplication.
1. VECTOR BUNDLES

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module (understood: of finite type). We set \(X=V(E)\), \(U=V(E)^*\) (complement of the zero section) and we denote by \(p:X\to S\) and \(q:U\to S\) the canonical projections.

PROPOSITION 1.1.— Let \(L\) be an \(A\)-Module.

(i) a) For every integer \(j>0\) we have \(R^jp_*(p^*L)=0\).

b) The adjunction morphism \(L\to p_*p^*(L)\) is an isomorphism.

(ii) We suppose \(E\) of constant rank \(r\).

a) \(R_Y^ip_*(p^*L)=0\) if \(i\ne2r\).

b) The trace morphism (SGA 4 XVIII)

\[R_Y^{2r}p_*(p^*L)\longrightarrow L\otimes\mu_S^{\otimes-r}\]

is an isomorphism.
Proof: Part (i) follows immediately, by localization for the Zariski topology, from (SGA 4 XV 2.2). Assertion (ii) is essentially contained in (SGA 4 XVIII).

COROLLARY 1.2.— Let \(L\) be a \(A_S\)-Module. For every integer \(i\), the inverse image morphism
\[H^i(S,L)\longrightarrow H^i(X,p^*L)\]

is an isomorphism.
Proof: By (1.1), the adjunction morphism \(L\to Rp_*(p^*L)\) is an isomorphism. As \(H^*(X,p^*L)\simeq H^*(S,Rp_*p^*(L))\), the assertion follows.

PROPOSITION 1.3.— Suppose \(E\) of constant rank \(r\). For every \(A_S\)-Module \(L\), the following properties hold:

(i) a) \(R^iq_*(q^*L)=0\) if \(i\ne0,,2r-1\).

b) The adjunction morphism \(L\to q_*q^*(L)\) is an isomorphism.

c) Denoting by \(Y\) the complement of \(U\) in \(X\), the morphism

\[R^{2r-1}q_*(q^*L)\longrightarrow L\otimes\mu_S^{\otimes-r}\]

composite of the "boundary" morphism,

\[R^{2r-1}q_*(q^*L)\longrightarrow R_Y^{2r}p_*(p^*L)\tag{1}\]

and of the purity isomorphism (SGA 4 XVIII)

\[R_Y^{2r}p_*(p^*L)\longrightarrow L\otimes\mu_S^{\otimes-r}\]

is an isomorphism.

(ii) a) \(R_!^iq(q^*L)=0\) if \(i\ne(1,2r)\).

b) The boundary morphism \(L\longrightarrow R_!^1q(q^*L)\) coming from the unbounded exact sequence \((f:Y\longrightarrow S\ \text{can. proj.})\)

\[\longrightarrow R_!^iq(q^*L)\to R_!^ip(p^*L)\to R_!^if_*(f^*L)\to R_!^{i+1}q(q^*L)\to\cdots\]

is an isomorphism.

c) The trace morphism

\[R_!^{2r}q(q^*L)\longrightarrow L\otimes\mu_S^{\otimes-r}\]

is an isomorphism.
Proof: Let \(\alpha:U\longrightarrow X\) and \(\beta:Y\longrightarrow X\) be the canonical immersions. We know (SGA 4 XVI 3.7) that, setting \(F=p^*(L)\), we have:
\[F\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\alpha_*\alpha^*(F)\quad\text{and}\quad (R^j\alpha_*)\alpha^*(F)=0\quad\text{if }j\ne0,2r-1.\]

Moreover the sheaves \((R^j\alpha_*)(\alpha^*F)\) for \(j\geq1\) are supported in \(Y\), hence acyclic for the functor \(p_*\). From these remarks we deduce assertions (i) a) and b) by using the Leray spectral sequence
\[R^ip_*(R^j\alpha_*)(q^*L)\Rightarrow R^iq_*(q^*L).\]
Assertion (i) c) follows from (1.1 (ii)) and from the unbounded exact sequence
\[\longrightarrow R_Y^ip_*(p^*L)\to R^ip_*(p^*L)\to R^iq_*(q^*L)\to R_Y^{i+1}p_*(p^*L)\to\cdots\]
(*)

Let us prove (ii). Assertion c) comes from the fact that the morphism \(q\) is smooth and has its geometric fibers connected of dimension \(r\). The exact sequence mentioned in b) is obtained by applying the functor \(R_!p\) to the canonical exact sequence
\[0\to\alpha_!(q^*L)\to p^*L\to\beta_!(\beta^*p^*L)\to0.\]
Using (1.1 (ii)), we deduce without difficulty from this unbounded exact sequence the assertions ii) a) and b).

Remark 1.4. For \(S=\operatorname{spec}(\mathbb C)\), (1.3) expresses that the complement of the origin in \(\mathbb C^r\) has the same cohomology as the real sphere of dimension \(2r-1\), a deformation retract of \(\mathbb C^r-0\).

COROLLARY 1.5. (Exact sequence of GYSIN)— The Leray spectral sequence
\[H^i(S,R^jq_*(q^*L))\Rightarrow H^{i+j}(U,q^*L)\]

defines an unbounded exact sequence (called the Gysin exact sequence)

\[\cdots\to H^i(S,L)\to H^i(U,q^*L)\to H^{i-2r+1}(S,L\otimes\mu_S^{\otimes-r})\to H^{i+1}(S,L)\to\cdots\]
Proof: The two-row lemma.

footnotes.html:
The sheaf \(R_Y^{2r}p_*(p^*L)\) is associated to the presheaf
\[U\longmapsto H_{Y\times_S U}^{2r}(X\times_S U,p^*L).\]

blocks.html: 2. PROJECTIVE SCHEMES

In what follows, the mode of exposition adopted is based on a suggestion of L. Illusie, and the method followed is close to a method of P. Deligne for proving the degeneration of certain spectral sequences [6].
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two schemes and \(f:X\to Y\) a morphism. We are going to define an arrow
\[(2.1.1)\qquad Rf_*(A_X)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}Rf_*(A_X)\longrightarrow Rf_*(A_X)\]

of \(D(Y)\), having the usual associativity and commutativity properties, and which therefore equips \(Rf_*(A_X)\) with a structure of "generalized algebra". To this end, let us denote by \(C^*(X,A_X)\)
the Godement resolution of \(A_X\) (SGA 4 XVII). Proceeding as in (TF 6.6), we define a morphism of resolutions
\[C^*(X,A_X)\otimes C^*(X,A_X)\longrightarrow C^*(X,A_X\otimes A_X).\]
Composing it with the morphism
\[C^*(X,A_X\otimes A_X)\longrightarrow C^*(X,A_X)\]
deduced from the multiplication of \(A_X\), we finally obtain a morphism of
\[(2.1.2)\qquad C^*(X,A_X)\otimes C^*(X,A_X)\longrightarrow C^*(X,A_X).\]
Applying the functor \(f_*\), we deduce from it without difficulty a morphism of complexes of \(A_Y\)-Modules
\[f_*C^*(X,A_X)\otimes f_*C^*(X,A_X)\longrightarrow f_*C^*(X,A_X),\]
whence immediately (2.1.1) by using flat resolutions of the components of the left-hand expression.

Let \(S\) be a scheme and \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of rank \(r+1\). We denote by \(P=P_S(E)\xrightarrow{p}S\) the corresponding projective bundle. The canonical \(\mathcal O_P\)-Module \(\mathcal O_P(1)\) defines, as is well known, thanks to the Kummer exact sequence, an element
and we shall denote by \(\eta\) the image of \(\xi\) by the canonical morphism
\[H^2(P,\mu)\longrightarrow H^0(S,R^2p_*(\mu))\]
coming from the Leray spectral sequence
\[H^i(S,R^jp_*(\mu))\Rightarrow H^{i+j}(P,\mu).\]
The class \(\xi\) can be identified with an arrow \(A_P\to\mu_P[2]\) of \(D(P)\), which defines by transposition \(\mu_P^{\otimes-1}[-2]\to A_P\), whence by the usual adjunction an arrow
\[c:\mu_S^{\otimes-1}[-2]\longrightarrow Rp_*(A_P),\]
coinciding with \(\eta\) on the cohomology of dimension 2.
By tensor product the arrow \(c\) defines, thanks to (2.2.1), arrows
\[c_i:\mu_S^{\otimes-i}[-2i]\longrightarrow Rp_*(A_P),\]
for every integer \(i\geq1\), and we agree to denote by \(c_0\) the adjunction arrow \(A_S\to Rp_*(A_P)\). We thus define an arrow
\[\gamma=\bigoplus_{i=0}^r c_i:\bigoplus_{i=0}^r\mu_S^{\otimes-i}[-2i]\longrightarrow Rp_*(A_P)\]
THEOREM 2.2.1.— The arrow \(\gamma\) is an isomorphism.
Once the arrow \(\gamma\) has been defined, (2.2.1) is equivalent to its following corollary:

PROPOSITION 2.2.2.— Under the preceding hypotheses, we have:

a) \(R^{2i+1}p_*(A_P)=0\) for every \(i\in\mathbb N\).

b) When we equip \(\bigoplus_i R^{2i}p_*(\mu_P^{\otimes i})\) with its \(A_S\)-Algebra structure deduced from the cup-product, the morphism of \(A_S\)-Algebras

\[A_S[T]\longrightarrow\bigoplus_iR^{2i}p_*(\mu_P^{\otimes i})\]

defined by \(\eta\) has for kernel the ideal \((T^{r+1})\) and therefore defines an isomorphism of \(A_S\)-Algebras

\[\theta:A_S[T]/(T^{r+1})\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\bigoplus_iR^{2i}p_*(\mu_P^{\otimes i}).\]

c) The morphism

\[A_S\longrightarrow R^{2r}p_*(\mu^{\otimes r})\]

obtained by composing \(\theta\) and the restriction to \(A_S\) of multiplication by \(T^r\) of \(A_S[T]/(T^{r+1})\) into itself is the inverse of the Trace morphism (SGA 4 XVIII)

\[R^{2r}p_*(\mu^{\otimes r})\longrightarrow A_S\,;\]

in other words, we have

\[\operatorname{Tr}(\eta^r)=1.\]

Proof: The data being stable under base change, the proper base change theorem (SGA 4 XII 5.1), and the compatibility of the trace morphism with base change (SGA 4 XVIII) allow us to reduce to verifying the analogous assertion for the fibers of \(p\). In other words, we may suppose that \(S\) is the spectrum of a separably closed field \(k\) and that \(p\) is the canonical morphism \(\mathbb P_k^r\to k\). In this case, the proof uses the following lemma.

LEMMA 2.2.3.— Let \(k\) be a separably closed field and \(p:\mathbb P_k^r\to k\) the canonical projection. Then:

a) \(R^{2i+1}p_*(A_P)=0\) for every \(i\).

b) \(R^{2i}p_*(A_P)\simeq A_k\) for \(i\in[0,r]\), \(=0\) if \(i>r\).

The lemma, manifestly true for \(r=0\), is proved by induction on \(r\). Suppose then that it is true for \(r-1\) and let us prove it for \(r\). The projective space \(\mathbb P_k^{r-1}\) embeds as a closed set in \(\mathbb P_k^r\) with complement the open set \(\mathbb E_k^r\), whence an exact sequence of cohomology with proper supports
\[\cdots\to H_!^i(\mathbb E_k^r)\to H^i(\mathbb P_k^r)\to H^i(\mathbb P_k^{r-1})\to H_!^{i+1}(\mathbb E_k^r)\to\cdots\]
The computation of the cohomology with proper supports of \(\mathbb E_k^r\) (1.1) shows that if \(i\ne(2r,2r-1)\), the inverse image
\[H^i(\mathbb P_k^r)\longrightarrow H^i(\mathbb P_k^{r-1})\]

is an isomorphism, whence the assertion in this case. If \(i=2r-1\), we see similarly that \(H^{2r-1}(\mathbb P_k^r)\) is contained in \(H^{2r-1}(\mathbb P_k^{r-1})\), hence null by the induction hypothesis. Finally, if \(i=2r\), the relations
show that \(H_!^{2r}(\mathbb E_k^r)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}H^{2r}(\mathbb P_k^r)\), whence the assertion by (1.1).
The lemma implies part a) of 2.2.1. Let us now prove that \(\theta\) is an isomorphism. The morphism \(\theta\) can be interpreted as a ring morphism
\[A[T]/(T^{r+1})\longrightarrow\bigoplus_i H^{2i}(P,\mu^{\otimes i})\]

so that, by (2.2.3), the assertion will be proved if we show that for every \(i\in[0,r]\), \(\xi^i\) is a free element of the \(A\)-module \(H^{2i}(P,\mu^{\otimes i})\). For this it suffices to see it for \(\xi^r\), which will be an immediate consequence of 2.2.2 c).

Let us then prove part c). In practice, this is the definition of the trace morphism in this situation; in any case, it expresses the compatibility of the trace morphism and the fundamental class in the diagram below
\[\xymatrix{x_0\ar[r]^{\mathrm{inj.}}\ar[dr]&\mathbb P_k^r\ar[d]^p\\&k}\]
where \(\mathrm{inj}:x_0\longrightarrow\mathbb P_k^r\) is the injection of a closed point of \(\mathbb P_k^r\).

COROLLARY 2.2.4.— Let \(L\) be a complex of \(A_S\)-Modules. The arrow
\[R\Gamma\bigl(S,\gamma\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}\operatorname{id}(L)\bigr): \bigoplus_{i=0}^r R\Gamma(S,L\otimes\mu_S^{\otimes i})[-2i]\longrightarrow R\Gamma(P,p^*L)\]

is an isomorphism.
Indeed, it suffices to apply the functor \(R\Gamma_S\) to the isomorphism \(\gamma\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}\operatorname{id}_L\).
2.2.5. To state the following corollary, we shall set for every scheme \(X\)
\[A^*(X)=\bigoplus_i H^{2i}(X,\mu_X^{\otimes i})\]
and we shall use this notation systematically in the sequel.

COROLLARY 2.2.6.— The inverse image homomorphism
\[p^*:A^*(S)=\bigoplus_iH^{2i}(S,\mu_S^{\otimes i})\longrightarrow \bigoplus_iH^{2i}(P,\mu_P^{\otimes i})=A^*(P)\]

is an injective morphism of unitary rings, and the elements \(1,\xi,\ldots,\xi^r\) form a basis of the \(A^*(S)\)-module \(A^*(P)\). In particular, \(A^*(P)\) is a free \(A^*(S)\)-module of the same rank as \(E\).
3. CHERN CLASSES

Let \(S\) be a scheme and \(E\) a \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module locally free of constant rank \(r\). Denoting by \(\check E\) the dual of \(E\), we set \(P=P_S(\check E)\). We have seen (2.2.6) that, denoting by \(\xi\) the class of \(\mathcal O_P(1)\) in \(H^2(P,\mu)\), the \(A^*(S)\)-module \(A^*(P)\) is free with basis \(1,\xi,\ldots,\xi^{r-1}\). In particular, it is immediate for reasons of homogeneity that there exists a unique relation of the form
\[(3.1)\qquad \xi^r+c_1\xi^{r-1}+\cdots+c_i\xi^{r-i}+\cdots+c_r=0,\]
with \(c_i\in H^{2i}(S,\mu^{\otimes i})\) for every \(i\in[0,r]\).

Now let \(E\) be an arbitrary locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module. For every integer \(r\), the set \(S_r\) of the points of \(S\) at which \(E\) has rank \(r\) is an open set. We thus obtain a partition
\[S=\coprod_r S_r\]
of \(S\) into disjoint open sets; in particular, for every \(A_S\)-Module \(M\) and every integer \(i\), the product morphism of the inverse images
\[H^i(S,M)\longrightarrow\prod_r H^i(S_r,M/S_r)\]
is a bijection.
The preceding considerations allow us to justify the following definition.

DEFINITION 3.2.— Let \(S\) be a scheme and \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module. For every integer \(i\geq0\), we call \(i^{\text{th}}\) Chern class of \(E\), and denote by \(c_i(E)\), the element of \(H^{2i}(S,\mu^{\otimes i})\) whose restriction, for every integer \(r\geq0\), to the open set \(S_r\) of \(S\) formed of the points where \(E\) has rank \(r\) is the element \(c_i\) defined by relation 3.1. If the rank of \(E\) is bounded, we call total Chern class of \(E\) the element
\[c(E)=\sum_i c_i(E)\]

of \(A^*(S)\); in the general case, we denote in this way the corresponding element in

\[\widehat A^*(S)=\prod_i A^i(S).\]
3.3. It is evident that whatever \(S\) and \(E\) may be, \(c_0(E)=1\), and that if the rank of \(E\) is bounded by \(d\), then \(c_i(E)=0\) for \(i>d\).

PROPOSITION 3.4.— The Chern classes previously defined satisfy the following properties

(i) (Normalization) Let \(L\) be an invertible \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module. Denoting by \([L]\) the image of the class of \(L\) by the boundary morphism

\[H^1(S,\mathbb G_m)\longrightarrow H^2(S,\mu),\]

we have

\[c(L)=1+[L].\]

(ii) (Stability under base change). Let \(f:S'\to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module. For every integer \(i\), we have

\[f^*(c_i(E))=c_i(f^*E).\]

(iii) (Additivity). Let \(0\to E'\to E\to E''\to0\) be an exact sequence of locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules. For every integer \(n\), we have the relation

\[c_n(E)=\sum_{i+j=n}c_i(E')c_j(E'').\]
Before beginning the proof, let us extract the following corollary.

COROLLARY 3.5.— For every exact sequence \(0\to E'\to E\to E''\to0\) of locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules, we have
\[c(E)=c(E')\,c(E'')\]

in \(\widehat A^*(S)\).
Proof of 3.4. (following A. Grothendieck: La théorie des classes de Chern. Bull. S.M.F. (1958), pp. 137–154).
(i) Let \(E\) be a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module; we set \(P=P_S(\check E)\) and we denote by \(p:P\to S\) the canonical projection. We recall that there is a canonical surjective homomorphism
\[p^*(\check E)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P(1).\]
In particular if \(E\) is invertible, setting \(E=L\) in accordance with the notations of (i), we have \(P=S\) and an isomorphism
\[L\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\mathcal O_P(1).\]
Consequently, \(\xi+[L]=0\) and the assertion follows from the definition of the Chern classes.
(ii) Immediate verification left to the reader.

(iii) Using a partition of \(S\) into open sets on which the Modules under consideration have constant rank, we may suppose that \(E,E',E''\) are of constant rank equal to \(r,r',r''\) respectively. Suppose then the following lemma proved, and let us show how it implies the assertion.

LEMMA 3.6.— Let \(E\) be a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module equipped with a finite filtration
\[0=E_0\subset E_1\subset\cdots\subset E_i\subset\cdots\subset E_r=E\]

whose successive quotients \(L_i=E_i/E_{i-1}\) \((1\leq i\leq r)\) are invertible. Then the equality below holds:

\[c(E)=(1+[L_1])(1+[L_2])\cdots(1+[L_r]).\]

Let \(h:Z\to S\) be the fiber product of the flag schemes of \(E'\) and \(E''\). It is clear by definition of the flag schemes that \(h^*(E')\) and \(h^*(E'')\) admit finite filtrations with invertible quotients \((L_i')\) \((1\leq i\leq r')\) and \((L_j'')\) \((1\leq j\leq r'')\) respectively. It follows that \(h^*(E)\) admits a filtration whose successive quotients are the \(L_i'\) and the \(L_j''\). The application of lemma 3.6 then shows that
\[c(h^*(E))=\prod_{1\leq i\leq r'}c(L_i')\prod_{1\leq j\leq r''}c(L_j'') =c(h^*(E'))c(h^*(E'')).\]
that is, thanks to (iii)
But the morphism \(h\) is a composite of morphisms of the type
\[P_T(F)\longrightarrow T\qquad(F\ \text{loc. free }\mathcal O_T\text{-Module}),\]
hence (2.2.5) the inverse image homomorphism \(h^*:A^*(S)\to A^*(Z)\) is an injection. Whence the desired assertion thanks to (3.5.1).
Let us prove lemma 3.6. By the preceding argument, it suffices to see the analogous relation

where, let us recall, \(p:P=P_S(\check E)\to S\) is the canonical projection. For every \(i\in[1,r]\) the monomorphism \(E_i\to E\) defines by duality an epimorphism \(\check E\to\check E_i\); setting \(Y_i=P_S(\check E_i)\), we obtain a sequence of closed subschemes of \(P\):
\[S\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}Y_1\subset Y_2\subset\cdots\subset Y_i\subset\cdots\subset Y_r=P.\]
Let us also denote for every pair \((i,j)\) of integers with \(1\leq i\leq j\leq r\)
\[\alpha_{ji}:Y_i\to Y_j\]

the canonical closed immersion, and let us set \(\alpha_i=\alpha_{r,i}\). For every integer \(i\in[1,r-1]\), it follows from the evident lemma (3.6.1) below that \(Y_i\) is defined in \(Y_{i+1}\) by an invertible ideal isomorphic to
\[\alpha_{i+1}^*(p^*(\check L_{i+1})\otimes\mathcal O_P(-1)).\]

LEMMA 3.6.1.— Let \(0\to L\to E\to F\to0\) be an exact sequence of locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules. Then the sequence
\[0\to L\otimes_S S(E)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{u}}S(E)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{v}}S(F)\to0,\]

in which \(v\) denotes the evident morphism and \(u\) is the morphism of degree one corresponding to the inclusion of \(L\) in \(E\), is an exact sequence of graded \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules.
Moreover, \(Y_1\) can be identified with \(S\) and we have an isomorphism
\[\alpha_1^*(p^*(\check L_1))\simeq\mathcal O_{Y_1}(1),\quad\text{that is}\] \[\alpha_1^*(p^*(\check L_1)\otimes\mathcal O_P(-1))\simeq\mathcal O_{Y_1}.\tag{3.6.2.}\]

We shall be able to conclude by using the following two properties of the Gysin homomorphism (SGA 4 XVIII) associated to a closed immersion \(u:Z\to X\) of smooth \(S\)-schemes defined by an Ideal \(J\) of \(\mathcal O_X\):
(3.6.3) It satisfies the projection formula
(3.6.4) If the ideal \(J\) is invertible, we have the relation
Indeed, we shall see from this, by increasing induction on the integer \(i\), that we have
\[(\alpha_i)_*(\alpha_i)^*\left(\prod_{j\leq i}[p^*(\check L_j)\otimes\mathcal O_P(-1)]\right)=0.\tag{3.6.5}\]
In the case \(i=r\), assertion (3.6.5) reads
\[\prod_i[p^*(\check L_i)\otimes\mathcal O_P(-1)]=0,\]
or again
whence immediately (3.6). Assertion (3.6.5) comes, for \(i=1\), from (3.6.2). Suppose it true for \(i\) and let us show that it is true for \(i+1\). Let us set for simplicity
\[N=\prod_{j\leq i}[p^*(\check L_j)\otimes\mathcal O_P(-1)].\]
Equality (3.6.5) for \(i\) can also be written
that is, taking into account the projection formula for \(\alpha_{i+1,i}\),
\[(\alpha_{i+1})_*[(\alpha_{i+1})^*(N).(\alpha_{i+1})^*(p^*(\check L_{i+1})\otimes\mathcal O_P(-1))]=0,\]
whence immediately the desired result.

COROLLARY 3.7

(i) For every locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module \(E\) and every integer \(i\), we have

\[c_i(\check E)=(-1)^i c_i(E).\]

(ii) If \(E\) is a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of rank \(r\), we have

\[c_1(E)=c_1(\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^r E).\]

(iii) More generally, the Chern classes of a tensor product or of an exterior power can be expressed by means of the universal polynomials made explicit in (SGA 6 V 6.1).

Proof: Let us prove for example (ii), the other assertions being seen in an analogous way. Using the flag scheme of \(E\), we reduce to the case where \(E\) admits a filtration with invertible quotients \(L_i\). Then the assertion follows from the well-known isomorphism
\[\mathop{\bigwedge}\nolimits^r(E)\simeq\mathop{\bigotimes}_i(L_i).\]

3.8. Given a scheme \(X\) locally of finite type over \(\operatorname{spec}(\mathbb C)\), the set \(X(\mathbb C)\) of its rational points is canonically endowed with a structure of locally compact topological space. We therefore have available, by way of algebraic topology, a theory of Chern classes [3], associating to every continuous \(\mathbb C\)-vector bundle \(V\) on \(X(\mathbb C)\) classes \(c_i(V)\in H^{2i}(X(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z)\). Now let \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb C}\) be the sheaf of continuous complex-valued functions on \(X(\mathbb C)\), and \(\mathcal E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb C}\)-Module of finite type; we define Chern classes
\[c_i(\mathcal E)\in H^{2i}(X(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z),\]

namely those of the \(\mathbb C\)-vector bundle of which \(\mathcal E\) is the sheaf of continuous sections. Finally, using the evident map \(\mathbb Z\to\mathbb Z/\nu\mathbb Z\), we deduce Chern classes, denoted in the same way if no confusion is possible
\[c_i(\mathcal E)\in H^{2i}(X(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z/\nu\mathbb Z).\tag{3.8.1}\]

Moreover, when we equip \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\) with its structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_a\) and \(X(\mathbb C)\) with the Ring \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb C}\), we have a morphism of ringed topoi (SGA 4 XI)
\[\varepsilon:X(\mathbb C)\to X_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\]
the structural morphism of Rings
\[\varepsilon^*(\mathcal O_a)\to\mathcal O_{\mathbb C}\tag{3.8.2}\]
coming from the fact that "every algebraic function is continuous". The morphism \(\varepsilon\) induces a morphism of abelian sheaves
\[\varepsilon^*(\mu_{X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}})\to\mu_{X(\mathbb C)},\tag{3.8.3}\]

the letter \(\mu\) denoting in each of the two contexts the sheaf of \(\nu\)-th roots of unity. Finally, we shall canonically identify \((\mathbb Z/\nu\mathbb Z)_{X(\mathbb C)}\) and \(\mu_{X(\mathbb C)}\) by means of the isomorphism
\[(\mathbb Z/\nu\mathbb Z)_{X(\mathbb C)}\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}}\mu_{X(\mathbb C)}\tag{3.8.4}\]
sending the section 1 to the section "\(e^{2i\pi/\nu}\)".

PROPOSITION 3.8.5.— Let \(E\) be a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module. Denoting for every integer \(i\geq0\) by
\[\varepsilon^*:H^{2i}(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\mu_X^{\otimes i})\to H^{2i}(X(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z/\nu\mathbb Z)\]

the inverse image morphism deduced from 3.8.3 and 3.8.4, we have the equality

\[c_i(\varepsilon^*E)=\varepsilon^*c_i(E).\]
Proof: It suffices to see it when \(E\) is invertible. Then, with the usual identifications, \(\varepsilon^*(E)\) is the image of \(E\) by the canonical map
\[\varepsilon^*:H^1(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\mathbb G_m)\to H^1(X(\mathbb C),\mathcal O_{\mathbb C}^*),\tag{3.8.6}\]
and it is a matter of seeing that the diagram

\[\xymatrix{H^1(X,\mathbb G_m)\ar[r]^{\varepsilon^*}\ar[d]_{\partial}&H^1(X(\mathbb C),\mathcal O_{\mathbb C}^*)\ar[d]^{c_1}\\H^2(X,\mu_X)\ar[r]^{\varepsilon^*}&H^2(X(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z/\nu\mathbb Z)}\tag{3.8.7}\]
in which \(\partial\) is the boundary morphism coming from the canonical exact sequence
\[0\to\mu_X\to\mathbb G_m\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\nu}}\mathbb G_m\to0\tag{3.8.8}\]
and \(c_1\) the first Chern class (3.8.1), is commutative. In fact, using the fact that the "first Chern class" morphism
\[H^1(X(\mathbb C),\mathcal O_{\mathbb C}^*)\to H^2(X,\mathbb Z)\]
is the boundary morphism of the canonical exact sequence
\[0\to\mathbb Z\to\mathcal O_{\mathbb C}\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\exp}}\mathcal O_{\mathbb C}^*\to0,\qquad t\mapsto2i\pi t,\]
we see easily that the morphism \(c_1\) of (3.8.7) is none other than the boundary morphism associated to the exact sequence
\[0 o\mathbb Z/ u\mathbb Z o\mathcal O_{\mathbb C}^*\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{ u}}\mathcal O_{\mathbb C}^* o0,\qquad t\mapsto e^{2i\pi t/ u},\]
which, taking into account (3.8.4), is the analogue of (3.8.8). The assertion follows immediately.

3.9. Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module. An immediate passage to the limit allows us to define, for every prime number \(\ell\) prime to the residue characteristics of \(X\), \(\ell\)-adic Chern classes
\[c_i(E)\in H^{2i}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\mathrel{\mathop{=}^{\mathrm{déf}}}\varprojlim_\nu H^{2i}(X,\mu_{\ell^\nu}^{\otimes i}),\]
which possess the usual properties made explicit in 3.4 and 3.7 and which, in the context of 3.8, are manifestly compatible (in an evident sense) with the Chern classes defined by topological means.
Tensoring with \(\mathbb Q_\ell\), we deduce \(\ell\)-adic Chern classes modulo torsion
\[c_i(E)\in H^{2i}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))=H^{2i}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell\]
which manifestly satisfy the same properties.

Suppose now that \(X\) is a quasi-projective scheme, smooth over a field \(k\), and denote here by \(C^*(X)\) its Chow ring (Sém. Chevalley: Anneaux de Chow et applications III 4). Recall that we have defined in IV a morphism of graded rings
\[\varphi_X:C^*(X)\longrightarrow A^*(X)\]
compatible with the direct image and inverse image morphisms. Moreover, the canonical isomorphism
\[\varepsilon_X:\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}}C^1(X)\]

associating to every invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(L\) the class of the divisor of a rational section of \(L\) which is null on no connected component of \(X\), gives rise, on the model of the aforementioned article of GROTHENDIECK (where \(k\) was supposed algebraically closed), to a theory of Chern classes with values in \(C^*(X)\). The composite morphism
\[\varphi_X\circ\varepsilon_X:\operatorname{Pic}(X)\longrightarrow H^2(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\]

is none other than the first \(\ell\)-adic Chern class. Indeed, it suffices to see it on the elements of \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) which are of the form \(\check J\), with \(J\) an invertible Ideal of \(\mathcal O_X\), and then this follows from (3.6.4). By the uniqueness properties of theories of Chern classes, we deduce that the morphism \(\varphi_X\) transforms Chern classes into Chern classes.

Remark 4.8. Let \(\ell\) be a prime number distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\). By simple passage to the projective limit, we deduce from 4.6 and 4.7 the corresponding statements in \(\ell\)-adic cohomology.

Now let \(\ell\) be a prime number and \(X\) a scheme proper over a separably closed field \(k\) of characteristic prime to \(\ell\). We recall (IV) that we call \(\ell\)-adic Euler-Poincaré characteristic of \(X\) the integer
\[\operatorname{EP}_{\ell}(X)=\sum_i(-1)^i\dim H^i(X,\mathbb Q_\ell).\]

COROLLARY 4.9.— Let \(X\) be a smooth, proper, connected scheme of dimension \(n\) over a separably closed field \(k\), with \(\operatorname{car}(k)\ne\ell\). Denoting by \(p:X\to k\) the canonical projection, we have
\[\operatorname{EP}_{\ell}(X)=p_*c_n(\check\Omega_X^1)\]

in \(\mathbb Q_\ell=H^0(k,\mathbb Q_\ell)\).
Proof: Let \(j:X\to X\times X\) be the diagonal immersion of \(X\) and \(q:X\times X\to k\) the canonical projection. It follows from 4.1 applied to the morphism \(j\) that
\[c_n(\check\Omega_X^1)=j^*j_*(1_X),\]
whence
\[p_*\bigl(c_n(\check\Omega_X^1)\bigr)=p_*j^*j_*(1_X)=q_*j_*j^*j_*(1_X).\]
Using the projection formula for the morphism \(j\), we deduce the equality
\[p_*\bigl(c_n(\check\Omega_X^1)\bigr)=q_*\bigl(j_*(1_X)\cup j_*(1_X)\bigr),\]
in which, by IV, the right-hand side is equal to the \(\ell\)-adic Euler-Poincaré characteristic of \(X\).

4.10. When \(X\) is projective over \(k\), the result recalled from IV shows that \(\operatorname{EP}_\ell(X)\) is, in the Chow ring of \(X\times X\), the self-intersection of the diagonal cycle, and consequently does not depend on the prime number \(\ell\ne\operatorname{car}(k)\). This will therefore allow us to speak of the Euler-Poincaré characteristic \(\operatorname{EP}(X)\) of \(X\), without reference to the integer \(\ell\).

PROPOSITION 4.11.— Let \(X\) be a smooth, projective, connected scheme of dimension \(n\) over a separably closed field \(k\). Then we have:
\[\operatorname{EP}(X)=\sum_{p,q}(-1)^{p+q}\dim H^p(X,\Omega_X^q).\]

Proof: Let us take up again the notations of the proof of 4.9, and let us denote by \(J\) the Ideal of the diagonal immersion of \(X\). As was pointed out in 4.10, \(\operatorname{EP}(X)\) is, in the Chow ring of \(X\times X\), the intersection product \(\Delta.\Delta\) of the diagonal cycle \(\Delta\) with itself. To compute it, we can neglect torsion, and consequently place ourselves in \(\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{top}}K(X)\). This shows that \(\operatorname{EP}(X)\) is the image under the direct image morphism
\[q_*:K(X\times X)\longrightarrow K(k)=\mathbb Z\]
of the square of the class of \(\mathcal O_{X\times X}/J\). Now by (SGA 6 VII 2.5), this last square is equal to
\[\sum_i(-1)^i\lambda^i([\Omega_X^1]),\]
whence immediately the desired formula, by definition of \(q_*\).
5. FLAG SCHEMES
Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of rank \(r\), \((p_i)_{1\leq i\leq m}\), \(m\) integers \(>0\) satisfying the equality
We recall that we call a flag of type \((p_1,\ldots,p_m)\) of \(E\) a finite filtration
\[0=E_m\subset E_{m-1}\subset\cdots\subset E_0=E\]
of \(E\) such that for every integer \(i\in[1,m]\) the quotient \(E_{i-1}/E_i\) is locally free of rank \(p_i\).

We define a functor \((\operatorname{Sch}/S)^\circ\to(\operatorname{Ens})\) by associating to every \(S\)-scheme \(u:T\to S\) the set of flags of type \((p_1,\ldots,p_m)\) of \(u^*(E)\) and to every morphism of \(S\)-schemes the inverse image, in an evident sense, on flags. It is proved (EGA I 2ème édition) that this functor is representable, and we call \(S\)-scheme of flags of type \((p_1,\ldots,p_m)\) of \(E\) any representative.

In the sequel, we shall denote by \(f:X\to S\) the \(S\)-scheme of flags of type \((p_1,\ldots,p_m)\) of \(E\). Moreover, the scheme of flags of type \((1,1,\ldots,1)\) of \(E\) will be denoted \(D\) and called the flag scheme of \(E\), without further precision, if no confusion is possible.
It is clear by definition that the \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(f^*(E)\) admits a canonical finite filtration
\[0=F_m\subset F_{m-1}\subset\cdots\subset F_0=f^*(E),\]
for which the quotient \(E_i=F_{i-1}/F_i\) \((1\leq i\leq m)\) is locally free of rank \(p_i\). Denoting for every integer \(i\in[1,m]\) by
\[g_i:D_i\longrightarrow X\]
the \(X\)-scheme of flags of \(E_i\) and by \(g:D_1\mathop{\times}_X\cdots\mathop{\times}_X D_m\to X\) the fiber product of the \(X\)-schemes \(D_i\), it is clear that the composite
\[(5.1)\qquad h=f\circ g:\mathop{\prod}_i{}_X D_i\longrightarrow S\]
is \(S\)-isomorphic to the \(S\)-scheme of flags of \(E\); up to \(S\)-isomorphism, we may therefore write \(D=\mathop{\prod}_i{}_X(D_i)\).

PROPOSITION 5.2.— We have, in \(D^b(S,A)\), an isomorphism
\[Rf_*(A)\simeq\bigoplus_i R^{2i}f_*(A)[-2i]\]
Proof: The morphism \(h\) being a composite of a finite number of morphisms of the type
\[(Q)\qquad P_T(F)\longrightarrow T\qquad(F\ \text{locally free }\mathcal O_T\text{-Module})\]
it follows from (2.2.1) that
\[Rh_*(A)\simeq\bigoplus_j R^jh_*(A)[-j].\]
For the same reason, we also have
\[Rg_*(A)\simeq\bigoplus_j R^jg_*(A)[-j].\]
Using ([6] 2.16), we deduce an isomorphism
\[Rf_*(g_*A)\simeq\bigoplus_j Rf_*(R^jg_*A)[-j].\]
As \(g\) is smooth with geometrically connected fibers, \(A_X\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}g_*(A_D)\), so that it remains for us to show that
\[(5.2.1)\qquad R^{2i+1}f_*(A)=0\qquad(i\in\mathbb N).\]

For this, we reduce immediately, thanks to the conservativity of the system of fiber functors (SGA 4 VIII 3.5), to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a separably closed field \(k\). Then, the morphism \(g\) being a composite of projective bundles, it follows from (2.2.4) that the inverse image
\[H^{2i+1}(X,A_X)\longrightarrow H^{2i+1}(D,A_D)\]

is a monomorphism, so that we may suppose that \(X=D\). In this case, as the projection morphism \(D\to k\) is itself a composite of projective bundles, the assertion follows by induction from (2.2.4).
COROLLARY 5.3.— For every morphism of schemes \(u:S\to T\), the Leray spectral sequence
\[E_2^{pq}=R^pu_*\,R^qf_*(A)\Rightarrow R^{p+q}(u\circ f)_*(A)\]
is degenerate.
Proof: cf. [6] 1.2.
We shall now make precise the multiplicative structure of the cohomology of \(X\), by first examining a particular case. With the preceding notations, we obtain a ring morphism
\[\alpha:A^*(S)[T_{i,j_i}]_{1\leq i\leq m,\,1\leq j_i\leq p_m}\longrightarrow A^*(X)\]

by sending \(A^*(S)\) into \(A^*(X)\) by the inverse image \(f^*\) and, for every pair \((i,j)\) as above, the element \(T_{i,j_i}\) to the \(j_i^{\text{th}}\) Chern class \(c_{j_i}(E_i)\). For every integer \(p\), we have by (3.4 (iii))

\[\varphi_X:\bigl(A^*(S)[T_{i,j_i}]_{(i,j_i)}\bigr)/J_X\longrightarrow A^*(X).\]

In the statement of the following proposition, we agree to set for every scheme \(Y\) and every \(A_Y\)-Module \(M\)
\[Q(Y,M)=\bigoplus_{n,i}H^n(Y,M\mathbin{\mathop\otimes\limits_{A_Y}}A_Y^{\otimes i}).\]
The \(A\)-module \(Q(Y,M)\) is canonically equipped with a structure of \(A^*(Y)\)-module, coming from the cup-product. If \(u:Z\to Y\) is a morphism of schemes, the inverse image morphism

PROPOSITION 5.5.— Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(E\) a locally free \(O_S\)-Module of rank \(r\). We denote by \(f:X\to S\) the \(S\)-scheme of flags of type \((p_1,\ldots,p_m)\) of \(E\). Then, for every \(A_S\)-Module \(L\), the canonical morphism
\[\alpha:A^*(X)\mathbin{\mathop\otimes\limits_{A^*(S)}}Q(S,L)\longrightarrow Q(X,f^*L)\]
is an isomorphism.

Proof: When \(X=P_S(E)\), this is an immediate consequence of (2.2.4) and (2.2.6). We conclude that it is true for \(X=D\). In the general case, as the canonical morphisms \(g:D\to X\) and \(h:D\to S\) are composites of projective bundles, the canonical morphisms
\[A^*(D)\mathbin{\mathop\otimes\limits_{A^*(S)}}Q(S,L)\longrightarrow Q(D,h^*L)\]
and
\[A^*(D)\mathbin{\mathop\otimes\limits_{A^*(X)}}Q(X,g^*L)\longrightarrow Q(D,g^*L)\]
are isomorphisms. Consequently \(\operatorname{id}_{A^*(D)}\otimes\alpha\) is an isomorphism, whence the result since \(A^*(D)\) is free over \(A^*(X)\).
We shall now give a relative variant of (5.4) and (5.5). With the preceding notations, let \(u:S\to T\) be a morphism of schemes. We set
\[A^*(X/T)=\bigoplus_i R^{2i}(u\circ f)_*(A_X^{\otimes i})\]
and likewise \(A^*(S/T)=\bigoplus_i R^{2i}u_*(A_S^{\otimes i})\). Finally, for every \(A_S\)-Module \(L\), we set
\[Q(S/T,L)=\bigoplus_{n,i}R^{2i}u_*\bigl(L\otimes\mu_S^{\otimes i}\bigr)\]
and
\[Q(X/T,f^*L)=\bigoplus_{n,i}R^n(u\circ f)_*\bigl(f^*L\otimes\mu_X^{\otimes i}\bigr).\]
For every pair \((i,j)\) of integers, with \(1\leq i\leq m\), \(1\leq j\leq p_i\), the image of the Chern class \(c_j(E_i)\) by the canonical morphism
\[H^{2j}(X,\mu_X^{\otimes j})\longrightarrow H^0\bigl(T,R^{2j}(u\circ f)_*(\mu_X^{\otimes j})\bigr)\]
defines a \(A_T\)-morphism.
\[c_{ij}:A_T\longrightarrow R^{2j}(u\circ f)_*(\mu_X^{\otimes j}).\]
The data of the \(c_{ij}\) and the inverse image \(f^*\) allow us to define a morphism of \(A^*(S/T)\)-Algebras
\[\theta_1:A^*(S/T)[T_{i,j_i}]_{(i,j_i)}\longrightarrow A^*(X/T).\]
Denoting by \(c_i\) the sections of \(A^*(S/T)\) defined by the Chern classes \(c_i(E)\), we see as in (5.4) that \(\theta_1\) vanishes on the ideal \(J\) generated locally by the relations
\[\sum_{i_1+\cdots+i_m=p}T_{1,i_1}\cdots T_{m,i_m}=c_p\qquad(p\in\mathbb N).\]
Whence a morphism of \(A^*(S/T)\)-Algebras
\[\theta:A^*(S/T)[T_{i,j_i}]_{(i,j_i)}/J\longrightarrow A^*(X/T).\]
PROPOSITION 5.6.— With the preceding notations:
a) The morphism of \(A^*(S/T)\)-Algebras
\[\theta:A^*(S/T)[T_{i,j_i}]_{(i,j_i)}/J\longrightarrow A^*(X/T)\]
is an isomorphism.
b) For every \(A_S\)-Module \(L\), the canonical morphism of \(A^*(X/T)\)-Modules
\[A^*(X/T)\mathbin{\mathop\otimes\limits_{A^*(S/T)}}Q(S/T,L)\longrightarrow Q(X/T,f^*L)\]
is an isomorphism.
Proof: Simple sheafification of (5.4) and (5.5) respectively.
6. GROUP SCHEMES
Let us begin with a general property of objects twisted by torsors.

PROPOSITION 6.1.— Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(G\) a quasi-compact \(S\)-group scheme, smooth and with connected fibers, and \(X\) an \(S\)-scheme equipped with a left action of \(G\). If \(f:E\to S\) is a (right) \(S\)-torsor under \(G\), we denote by \(X_E\) the \(S\)-scheme obtained from \(X\) by twisting by means of \(E\) and the action of \(G\) on \(X\). We denote by \(p:X\to S\) and \(q:X_E\to S\) the canonical projections. Under these conditions, for every \(A_S\)-Module \(M\) and every integer \(i\), the \(A_S\)-Module \(R^iq_*(q^*M)\) is canonically isomorphic to \(R^ip_*(p^*M)\).
Proof:

\[\begin{array}{ccccc} E\times_S X&\xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ }&X_E&&E\times_S X&\xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{pr}_2\ }&X\\ \downarrow\scriptstyle{\mathrm{pr}_1}&&\downarrow\scriptstyle q&&\downarrow\scriptstyle{\mathrm{pr}_1}&&\downarrow\scriptstyle p\\ E&\xrightarrow{\ f\ }&S&&E&\xrightarrow{\ f\ }&S \end{array}\]
\((D_1)\) \(\hspace{16em}(D_2)\)
We know that the diagram \((D_1)\), in which \(\varphi\) denotes the canonical quotient map, is cartesian. The morphism \(f\) being smooth, the smooth base change theorem yields an isomorphism
\[u:f^*R^iq_*(q^*M)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim}R^i(\mathrm{pr}_1)_*(\varphi^*q^*M).\]
Similarly, from the cartesian diagram \((D_2)\) we deduce an isomorphism
\[v:f^*R^ip_*(p^*M)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim}R^i(\mathrm{pr}_1)_*((\mathrm{pr}_2)^*p^*M).\]
But \(p\circ\mathrm{pr}_2=q\circ\varphi\), hence, identifying in a clear way \(\varphi^*q^*\) and \((\mathrm{pr}_2)^*p^*\), we define an isomorphism
\[v^{-1}\circ u:f^*R^iq_*(q^*M)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim}f^*R^ip_*(p^*M).\]
The morphism \(f\) having nonempty and connected geometric fibers, it follows from
it follows from (SGA 4 XII 6.5 (ii)) that the adjunction morphism \(\operatorname{adj}:\operatorname{id}\to f_*f^*\) is an isomorphism, so that
\[(\operatorname{adj})^{-1}\circ f_*(v^{-1}\circ u)\circ(\operatorname{adj})\]
is the desired isomorphism.

PROPOSITION 6.2.— (Mme RAYNAUD ([5])) Let \(S\) be a scheme and \(p:G\to S\) a reductive \(S\)-group scheme (SGA 3 XIX). For every locally constant constructible \(A_S\)-Module \(M\) and for every integer \(i\), the \(A_S\)-Module \(R^ip_*(p^*M)\) is locally constant and constructible. Moreover, for every morphism \(u:S'\to S\) defining a cartesian diagram
\[\xymatrix{G'\ar[r]^{u'}\ar[d]_{p'}&G\ar[d]^p\\S'\ar[r]_u&S}\]
the base change morphism
\[u^*R^ip_*(p^*M)\longrightarrow R^ip'_*(p\circ u')^*(M)\]
is an isomorphism.

Proof: The two assertions of the statement are local for the étale topology of \(S\). By (SGA 3 XII 2.3), we may therefore suppose that \(G\) is split. Let then \(B\) be a Borel subgroup of \(G\) of the type indicated in (SGA 3 XXII 5.1.1), so that the quotient \(G/B\) exists by (SGA 3 XXII 5.8.5), and \(q:G\to G/B\) and \(r:G/B\to S\) the canonical morphisms. The morphism \(r\) being proper and smooth, it satisfies the proper base change theorem and the functors \(R^ir_*\) transform locally constant constructible abelian sheaves into sheaves of the same type (SGA 4 XVI 2.2). Moreover, by (SGA 4 XXII 5.5.1), the \(S\)-scheme \(B\) is isomorphic to a finite product of vector bundles and punctured vector bundles, and consequently it follows immediately from (1.1) and (1.2) that, denoting by \(t:B\to S\) the canonical morphism, the

sheaves \(R^it_*(t^*M)\), with \(M\) a locally constant constructible \(A_S\)-Module, are locally constant constructible and stable under base change. Now let \(M\) be a locally constant constructible \(A_S\)-Module. It follows from the preceding considerations and from (6.1) that the sheaves \(R^iq_*(p^*M)\) are locally constant constructible, and we deduce, using the Leray spectral sequence
\[R^ir_*\bigl(R^jq_*\bigr)(p^*M)\Longrightarrow R^{i+j}p_*(p^*M)\]

that the same is true of the sheaves \(R^ip_*(p^*M)\). Finally, by (6.1), the sheaves \(R^iq_*(p^*M)\) are stable under base change; thanks to the proper base change theorem for \(r\), we deduce that the sheaves \(R^ip_*(p^*M)\) are also stable under base change.

Remark 6.3.— As every reductive \(S\)-group is, locally for the étale topology, the inverse image by the canonical morphism \(S\to\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb Z)\) of a reductive \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb Z)\)-scheme (SGA 3 XXIII 5.7 and XXV 1.3), (6.2) shows that the computation of the cohomology (with torsion coefficients prime to the characteristic) of reductive group schemes over an algebraically closed field reduces to the transcendental problem of the computation of the cohomology of reductive group schemes of the same type over the field of complex numbers, whose solution is known by the methods of algebraic topology, taking into account the comparison theorem (SGA 4 XI 4.4).

COROLLARY 6.4.— Let \(S\) be a scheme and \(p:G\to S\) an \(S\)-group scheme which is an extension of a finite étale \(S\)-group scheme \(F\) and a reductive \(S\)-group scheme \(H\). Then the conclusions of (6.2) are valid for every locally constant constructible \(A_S\)-Module \(M\).

Proof: After making the base change \(F\to S\) if necessary, we may suppose \(F\) of the form \(\Gamma_S\), where \(\Gamma\) denotes an ordinary finite group. Then we have an isomorphism of \(S\)-schemes
\[G\simeq\coprod_{\gamma\in\Gamma}H\]
and we conclude by (6.2).

COROLLARY 6.5.— Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(G\) a reductive \(S\)-group scheme and \(H\) a reductive \(S\)-subgroup scheme of \(G\), such that the quotient sheaf of \(G\) by \(H\) for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology is representable. Then, denoting by \(p:G/H\to S\) the canonical morphism, the conclusions of (6.2) are valid for every locally constant constructible \(A_S\)-Module \(M\).
Proof: Let \(f:G\to G/H\) be the canonical morphism, which is smooth, hence a descent morphism for étale sheaves (SGA 4 VIII 9.1). Let us set for every integer \(r\geq0\)
\[G_r=G\mathop{\times}_{G/H}G\mathop{\times}_{G/H}\cdots\mathop{\times}_{G/H}G\qquad(r+1\text{ times}),\]
which, by SGA 3 V 1.5, is canonically identified with
\[G\mathop{\times}_S\underbrace{H\mathop{\times}_S\cdots\mathop{\times}_S H}_{r\text{ times}},\]

and let us denote by \(f_r:G_r\to G/H\) and \(g_r:G_r\to S\) the canonical morphisms, so that in particular \(f_0=f\). Finally, for every integer \(q\geq0\), let us denote by \(\mathcal H^q(M)\) the complex
\[R^q(g_0)_*(f_0^*M)\to R^q(g_1)_*(f_1^*M)\to\cdots\to R^q(g_r)_*(f_r^*M)\to\cdots\]
By (SGA ), we have a descent (or Čech) spectral sequence
\[E(M):\quad E_2^{pq}=H^p(\mathcal H^q(M))\Longrightarrow E^n=R^np_*(M).\]

As the schemes \(G_r\) are reductive group schemes, it follows from 6.2 that the terms \(E_2^{pq}\) are locally constant constructible and compatible with every base change. We deduce immediately that the same is true for the \(E^n\).
7. COMPLETE INTERSECTIONS

In this section we suppose given a separably closed field \(k\). If \(\ell\) is a prime number \(\ne\operatorname{car}(k)\), we denote for every proper \(k\)-scheme \(X\) and every integer \(i\geq0\) by \(b_\ell^i(X)\) [or \(b^i(X)\) if no confusion is possible] the \(i\)-th Betti number of \(X\) for the coefficients \(\mathbb Z_\ell\). If \(X\) is projective over \(k\), we call hyperplane section of \(X\) the locus of zeros of a section transversal to the zero section of a vector bundle \(V(\mathcal L)\), with \(\mathcal L\) an ample invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module.

THEOREM 7.1.— (Lefschetz theorem) Let \(X\) be a projective smooth \(k\)-scheme, connected of dimension \(n\), \(Y\) a subscheme of \(X\) which is an intersection of \(d\) hyperplane sections of \(X\), and \(L\) a locally constant constructible \(A_X\)-Module. Then:
(i) The inverse image homomorphism
\[H^i(X,L)\to H^i(Y,L|Y)\]
is an isomorphism for \(i\leq n-d-1\), and a monomorphism for \(i=n-d\).
(ii) Suppose \(Y\) smooth over \(k\), of dimension \(n-d\). Then the Gysin homomorphism
\[H^i(Y,(L|Y)\otimes\mu_Y^{\otimes-d})\to H^{i+2d}(X,L)\]
is an isomorphism for \(i\geq n-d+1\) and an epimorphism for \(i=n-d\).

Proof: It is well known that the complement of a hyperplane section is an affine open set, and consequently \(U=X-Y\) is a union of \(d\) affine open sets. Assertion (i) then follows immediately from the exact cohomology sequence
\[\cdots\to H_c^i(U,L|U)\to H^i(X,L)\to H^i(Y,L|Y)\xrightarrow{\partial}H_c^{i+1}(U,L|U)\to\cdots\]
and from the following lemma.
LEMMA 7.1.1.— Let \(U\) be a \(k\)-scheme of finite type of dimension \(n\), a union of \(d\) affine open sets, and \(F\) a \(A_U\)-Module.
a) If \(F\) is constructible,
\[H^i(U,F)=0\qquad\text{for }i\geq n+d.\]
b) If \(F\) is locally constant and \(U\) smooth over \(k\),
\[H_c^i(U,F)=0\qquad\text{for }i\leq n-d.\]

For \(d=1\), assertion a) is none other than (SGA 4 XIV 3.2). In the general case, denoting by \(\mathcal U\) a covering of \(U\) by \(d\) affine open sets, we deduce a) thanks to the Čech spectral sequence
\[H^p(\mathcal U,\mathcal H^q(F))\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(U,F).\]

Let us prove b). As the functors \(H_c^i(U,\mathord\cdot)\) commute with filtered inductive limits (SGA 4 VII 3.3), we may suppose \(F\) constructible, and then the assertion follows from a), thanks to Poincaré duality (SGA 4 XVIII).
Finally, taking into account the definition of the Gysin morphism via the canonical isomorphism
\[(7.1.2)\qquad H^{i-2d}(Y,(L|Y)\otimes\mu_Y^{\otimes-d})\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}H_Y^i(X,L),\]
assertion (ii) follows from 7.1.1 a) and from the exact cohomology sequence associated to an open set and its complement
\[\cdots\to H_c^{i-1}(U,L|U)\xrightarrow{\partial}H_Y^i(X,L)\to H^i(X,L)\to H_c^i(U,L|U)\to\cdots.\]

Remark 7.1.3.— In fact, it follows from the excision property that the canonical isomorphism (7.1.2) generalizes to the case where the smoothness hypothesis on \(X\), and that of \(L\) being constructible locally constant, are satisfied only in a neighborhood of \(Y\), so that 7.1 (ii) generalizes in this setting.
For considerably more general formulations, see (SGA 2 XIV 4.6), which however is proved only granting resolution of singularities, hence provisionally in characteristic 0.

COROLLARY 7.2.— Let \(X\) be a projective smooth \(k\)-scheme, connected, of dimension \(n\), \(Y\) a subscheme of \(X\) which is an intersection of \(d\) hyperplane sections of \(X\), and \(L\) a constructible twisted constant \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf (\(\ell\ne\operatorname{car}(k)\)). Then:
(i) The inverse image homomorphism
\[\alpha_i:H^i(X,L)\to H^i(Y,L|Y)\]
is an isomorphism for \(i\leq n-d-1\), and a monomorphism for \(i=n-d\). If moreover \(L\) is locally free constructible, the cokernel of \(\alpha_{n-d}\) is torsion-free.
(ii) Suppose \(Y\) smooth over \(k\), of dimension \(n-d\). Then the Gysin homomorphism
is an isomorphism for \(i\geq n-d+1\) and an epimorphism for \(i=n-d\). If moreover \(L\) is locally free constructible, the epimorphism \(\beta_{n-d}\) is direct and its kernel is torsion-free.

Proof: Let us prove (i), assertion (ii) being deduced from it by Poincaré duality. In the general case, a simple passage to the limit starting from 7.1 (i) allows us to conclude. When \(L\) is a locally free \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf, we apply the following lemma to the inverse image morphism
\[R\Gamma(X,L)\to R\Gamma(Y,L|Y)\]
of \(D(\mathbb Z_\ell)\), hypothesis (iii) being satisfied thanks to 7.1 (i).
LEMMA 7.2.1.— Let \(V\) be a discrete valuation ring, \(k_V\) its residue field, \(u:K\to L\) a morphism of \(D(V)\).
We suppose that \(K\) and \(L\) have cohomology of finite type, and we denote by \(M\) the mapping-cylinder of \(u\). Then for every \(m\in\mathbb Z\) the following conditions are equivalent:
(i) \(H^p(M)=0\) for \(p\leq m-1\), and \(H^m(M)\) is torsion-free.
(ii) For every \(V\)-module \(N\), the morphism
\[H^p(u\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}\operatorname{id}_N):H^p(K\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}N)\to H^p(L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}N)\]
is an isomorphism for \(p\leq m-1\) and a monomorphism for \(p=m\).
(ii bis) For every \(V\)-module \(N\) we have
\[H^p(M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}N)=0\qquad(p\leq m-1).\]
(iii) The morphism
\[H^p(u\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}\operatorname{id}):H^p(K\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}k_V)\to H^p(L\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}k_V)\]
is an isomorphism for \(p\leq m-1\) and a monomorphism for \(p=m\).
(iii bis) \(H^p(M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}k_V)=0\) for \(p\leq m-1\).
Moreover, these equivalent conditions imply:
(i bis) The morphism
is an isomorphism for \(p\leq m-1\), and is a monomorphism with free cokernel for \(p=m\).
Proof: The following diagram of implications is evident
\[\begin{gathered}(ii)\Longleftrightarrow(ii\ \mathrm{bis})\\ \Downarrow\hspace{7em}(i)\Longrightarrow(i\ \mathrm{bis})\\ (iii)\Longleftrightarrow(iii\ \mathrm{bis})\end{gathered}\]

We shall show separately that \((i)\Rightarrow(ii\ \mathrm{bis})\) and \((iii\ \mathrm{bis})\Rightarrow(i)\), which will suffice to conclude. Assertion \((i)\Rightarrow(ii\ \mathrm{bis})\) is seen without difficulty from the Künneth exact sequence
\[0\to H^p(M)\otimes_VN\to H^p(M\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}N)\to\operatorname{Tor}_1^V(H^{p+1}(M),N)\to0.\]
The same exact sequence, for \(N=k_V\), shows that if (iii bis) is true, we have
\[H^p(M)\otimes_Vk_V=0\quad(p\leq m-1)\qquad\text{and}\qquad \operatorname{Tor}_1^V(H^m(M),k_V)=0.\]
We deduce (i) by Nakayama's lemma and the local criterion of flatness (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 10.1.3).

Under the preceding hypotheses, we see that the knowledge of the cohomology groups of \(X\) with coefficients in \(\mathbb Q_\ell\) implies the knowledge of those of \(Y\), except in dimension \(n-1\). The computation of the latter reduces immediately, under these conditions, to the computation of the Euler-Poincaré characteristic of \(Y\). It is this computation that we shall now be interested in, in a somewhat more general setting.

PROPOSITION 7.3.— Let \(X\) be a proper, smooth, connected \(k\)-scheme of dimension \(n\), \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module of constant rank \(r\), and \(Y\) the locus of zeros of a section of \(V(\check E)\) transversal to the zero section. Denoting by \(p:X\to k\) the canonical morphism and by \(p_*\) the corresponding Gysin morphism, we have the equality
\[ \operatorname{EP}(Y)=p_*\bigl(c(\check\Omega_X^1)c(E)^{-1}c_r(E)\bigr) \]
in \(H^0(k,\mathbb Q_\ell)=\mathbb Q_\ell\).

Proof: By (EGA IV 17.13.2), \(Y\) is smooth purely of dimension \(n-r\) and, denoting by \(i:Y\to X\) the canonical immersion, the conormal sheaf to \(i\) is the inverse image by \(i\) of the conormal sheaf \(\check E\) to the zero section of \(V(\check E)\). We therefore have an exact sequence
\[ (7.3.1)\qquad 0\to i^*(\check E)\to i^*(\Omega_X^1)\to\Omega_Y^1\to0, \]
from which immediately results the equality
\[ (7.3.2)\qquad c(\check\Omega_Y^1)=i^*\bigl(c(\check\Omega_X^1)c(E)^{-1}\bigr). \]
We deduce from this by (3.10) the following expression for \(\operatorname{EP}(Y)\)
\[ \operatorname{EP}(Y)=p_*i_*i^*\bigl(c(\check\Omega_X^1)c(E)^{-1}\bigr), \]
that is, taking into account the projection formula for the morphism \(i\),
\[ \operatorname{EP}(Y)=p_*\bigl(c(\check\Omega_X^1)c(E)^{-1}i_*(1_Y)\bigr). \]
The formula of the statement follows immediately thanks to (4.7).

COROLLARY 7.4.— Let \(X\) be a proper, smooth, connected \(k\)-scheme of dimension \(n\) and \(Y\) a smooth closed subscheme of dimension \(n-1\) of \(X\), hence defined by a section of an invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(L\). Denoting by \(p\) the canonical morphism of \(X\) into \(k\) and by \(p_*\) the corresponding Gysin morphism, we have the equality
\[ (7.4.1)\qquad \operatorname{EP}(X)-\operatorname{EP}(Y)=p_*\bigl(c(\check\Omega_X^1)c(L)^{-1}\bigr) \]
in \(H^0(k,\mathbb Q_\ell)=\mathbb Q_\ell\). Moreover
Proof: Equality (7.4.1) comes from 7.3 and 4.2, taking into account the evident equality
Using (7.2), we see that
\[ b^{n-1}(Y)=b^{n-1}(X)-b^n(X)+b^{n+1}(X)+(-1)^n(\operatorname{EP}(X)-\operatorname{EP}(Y)). \]
But \(b^{n-1}(X)=b^{n+1}(X)\) by Poincaré duality, whence (7.4.2).

COROLLARY 7.5.— Let \(X\) be a smooth, connected subscheme of dimension \(n\) of \(\mathbb P_k^{n+r}\), a global complete intersection of \(r\) divisors of \(\mathbb P_k^{n+r}\), say \(X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_r\). For every integer \(i\in(1,r)\) we denote by \(d_i\) the degree of \(X_i\). Then:
(i) The cohomology groups \(H^p(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\) are free \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules of finite type.
(ii) If \(p\) is an integer \(\leq2n\) and \(\ne n\),
\[ H^p(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\simeq \begin{cases}0&\text{if }p\text{ is odd},\\ \mathbb Z_\ell&\text{if }p\text{ is even}. \end{cases} \]
(iii) The Euler-Poincaré characteristic of \(X\) is given by the formula
\[ \operatorname{EP}(X)=d_1\cdots d_r \sum_{\substack{0\leq i\leq n\\j_1+\cdots+j_r=n-i}} (-1)^{n-i}{n+r+1\choose i}d_1^{j_1}\cdots d_r^{j_r}. \]

Proof: Assertion (i) follows immediately from (7.2). Likewise for (ii), taking into account the cohomology of projective spaces (no. 2). Let us prove part (iii). Let us denote by \(P\) the ambient projective space, by \(p:P\to k\) the canonical projection, by \(p_*\) the Gysin morphism

corresponding to it, and by \(\xi\) the class of \(\mathcal O_P(1)\). For every \(i\in(1,r)\), the scheme \(X_i\) is defined by a section of an invertible \(\mathcal O_P\)-Module \(L_i\), so that \(X\) is the locus of zeros of a section of \(V(\check L_1\oplus\cdots\oplus\check L_r)\) transversal to the zero section. Moreover we know that we have an exact sequence
\[ 0\to\Omega_P^1\otimes\mathcal O_P(1)\to\mathcal O_P^{n+r+1}\to\mathcal O_P(1)\to0, \]
whence
We then deduce from (7.3) the following expression for \(\operatorname{EP}(X)\).

\[ \begin{aligned} \operatorname{EP}(X)&=p_*\bigl(c(\check\Omega_P^1)c(\check L_1\oplus\cdots\oplus\check L_r)^{-1}c_1(\check L_1)\cdots c_1(\check L_r)\bigr)\\ &=p_*\bigl(d_1\cdots d_r\xi^r(1+\xi)^{n+r+1}(1+d_1\xi)^{-1}\cdots(1+d_r\xi)^{-1}\bigr). \end{aligned} \]
Using the fact that \(p_*(\xi^{n+r})=1\) (2.2.2 c)), we derive without difficulty the formula of the statement.

Remark 7.6. We can also obtain the expression of the Euler-Poincaré characteristic by using (4.11) and transcribing the computations of Hirzebruch (3, p.160-161) which use the Riemann-Roch theorem. For a more complete computation and the determination of the Hodge cohomology of complete intersections, we refer the reader to (SGA 7 XI).
By analogy with what happens in the transcendental case, where for constant coefficients we have another well-known theorem of Lefschetz ([4]), we are led to conjecture the following statement.(*)

If \(X\) is a projective, smooth and connected \(k\)-scheme of dimension \(n\) and \(\xi\) the class in \(H^2(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\) of a very ample invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module, then for every twisted constant \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaf \(L\) and every integer \(p\leq n\), the multiplication by \(\xi^{n-p}\):
is an isomorphism.
This conjectural statement has the following consequences, likewise not proved in char. \(\ne0\).
Let \(X\) be a projective smooth \(k\)-scheme, connected, of dimension \(n\), \(Y\) a smooth hyperplane section of \(X\), \(L\) a twisted constant \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaf on \(X\).
(i) The inverse image morphism
\[ H^p(X,L)\to H^p(Y,L|Y) \]
is an epimorphism for \(p\geq n\).
(ii) The Gysin morphism
is a monomorphism for \(p\leq n-2\).

Proof: Let \(j:X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r\) be a closed immersion and \(\xi_X\) (resp. \(\xi_Y\)) the class in \(H^2(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(1))\) (resp. \(H^2(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(1))\)) of \(\mathcal O_X(1)\) (resp. \(\mathcal O_Y(1)\)). We denote by \(\alpha:Y\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\) the canonical injection. For \(q\leq n-1\), the diagram
\[ \begin{CD} H^q(X,L) @>{\alpha^*}>> H^q(Y,L|Y)\\ @V{\cup\,\xi_X^{n-1-q}}VV @VV{\cup\,\xi_Y^{n-1-q}}V\\ H^{2n-2-q}(X,L(n-1-q)) @>{\alpha^*}>> H^{2n-2-q}(Y,(L|Y)(n-1-q)) \end{CD} \]

is commutative and the right-hand column is (conjecturally) an isomorphism. Let us set \(p=2n-2-q\); for \(q\leq n-2\), say \(p\geq n\), the top row is an isomorphism (7.2), whence assertion (i). Assertion (ii) is proved likewise by considering this time the following diagram, with \(p\leq n-1\).

\[ \begin{CD} H^p(Y,(L|Y)(-1)) @>{\mathrm{Gysin}}>> H^{p+2}(X,L)\\ @V{\cup\,\xi_Y^{n-1-p}}VV @VV{\cup\,\xi_X^{n-1-p}}V\\ H^{2n-2-p}(Y,(L|Y)(n-2-p)) @>{\mathrm{Gysin}}>> H^{2n-p}(X,L(n-1-p)) \end{CD} \]
in which the bottom row is an isomorphism for \(2n-2-p\geq n\), that is \(p\leq n-2\) (7.2 (iii)).

For a discussion of these conjectures and of their consequences, we refer the reader to the exposé of Kleiman (Algebraic cycles and the Weil conjectures, in "Dix exposés sur la cohomologie de schémas").

footnotes.html: As Deligne has observed, the statements (7.7) are false without an additional hypothesis on \(L\). However, Deligne has proved them (La conjecture de Weil II) when in addition \(L\) is "pure".

blocks.html: 8. BLOWN-UP VARIETIES

In this section, we give a cohomological variant of the computations for the group \(K\) and the "group of cycle classes" of a blown-up scheme, given in SGA 6 VII 3.7 and 4.8. The hypotheses and notations are those of the beginning of paragraph 4 (4.1 to 4.3). Under these conditions, the "fundamental class" morphism
\[ \gamma:j_*\bigl(\mu_{Y'}^{\otimes-1}\bigr)[-2]\longrightarrow A_{X'} \]
provides, after applying the functor \(Rf_*\), a morphism
\[ Rf_*(\gamma):j_*Rg_*\bigl(\mu_{Y'}^{\otimes-1}\bigr)[-2]\longrightarrow Rf_*(A_{X'}). \]

THEOREM 8.1.— The morphism of \(D^+(X,A)\)
\[ (8.1.1)\qquad A_X\oplus j_*Rg_*\bigl(\mu_{Y'}^{\otimes-1}\bigr)[-2] \longrightarrow Rf_*(A_{X'})\oplus j_*\bigl(\mu_Y^{\otimes-d}\bigr)[-2d] \]

defined by the matrix

\[ \begin{pmatrix} \mathrm{adj} & Rf_*(\gamma)\\ 0 & j_*(\mathrm{trace}) \end{pmatrix} \]

is an isomorphism.

For \(x\) belonging to \(Y\), we reduce, thanks to the proper base change theorem, to proving the following lemma (with \(r=d-1\)).

LEMMA 8.2.— Let \(k\) be a separably closed field, \(r\geq1\) an integer, and \(P=P_k^r\). Then:

a) The canonical map \(A\to H^0(P,A)\) is a bijection.

c) For \(0\leq p\leq2r-2\), multiplication by \(\xi=c_1(\mathcal O_P(1))\in H^2(P,\mu)\),

pages.title: 9. CHOW RING OF A BLOWN-UP VARIETY AND SELF-INTERSECTION FORMULA IN THE CHOW RING.

blocks.html: 9. CHOW RING OF A BLOWN-UP VARIETY AND SELF-INTERSECTION FORMULA IN THE CHOW RING.

In this paragraph we propose to give the proof, due to MUMFORD, of the statement analogous to 4.1 in the Chow ring. We shall then show how one can deduce from it the "key formula" (8.4.3), and consequently determine the structure of the Chow ring of a blown-up variety.

In accordance with the conventions of 3.9, we denote by \(C^*(X)\) the Chow ring of a scheme \(X\) which is quasi-projective and smooth over a field \(k\). The theory of Chern classes used is that of [1].
We shall make use of the following lemma several times.

LEMMA 9.1.— Let \(S\) be a quasi-projective and smooth scheme over a separably closed field \(k\), \(E\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of constant rank \(r+1\), and \(p:P=P_S(E)\longrightarrow S\) the associated projective bundle. We denote by \(F\) the \(\mathcal O_P\)-Module defined by the canonical exact sequence
\[(9.1.0)\qquad0\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow p^*(E)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P(1)\longrightarrow0,\]

and we set \(\xi=c_1(\mathcal O_P(1))\). Then:

(i) For every \(y\in C^*(S)\), we have

\[(9.1.1)\qquad y=p_*\bigl(p^*(y)c_r(\check F)\bigr).\]

(ii) Suppose that \(E=N\oplus\mathcal O_S\), with \(N\) a locally free \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of rank \(r\), so that \(P\) is identified with the projective completion \(\widehat N\) of \(V(N)\), and let us denote by

\[s:S\longrightarrow P_S(E)\]

the section composed of the zero section of \(V(N)\) and the canonical inclusion \(V(N)\hookrightarrow\widehat N\). Then:

(a) For every \(y\in C^*(S)\), we have the relations:

\[(9.1.2)\qquad s_*(y)=p^*(y)c_r(\check F)=p^*(y)\left(\sum_{i=0}^r p^*(c_i(\check N))\xi^{r-i}\right),\quad\text{in }C^*(\widehat N).\] \[(9.1.3)\qquad s^*s_*(y)=y\,c_r(\check N),\quad\text{in }C^*(S).\]

(b) For every \(z\in C^*(\widehat N)\), we have:

\[(9.1.4)\qquad s^*(z)=p_*\bigl(z\,c_r(\check F)\bigr),\quad\text{in }C^*(S).\]

Proof: Let us prove (9.1.1). Thanks to the projection formula for \(p\), it suffices to see that \(p_*(c_r(\check F))=1\). For this, one need only copy the proof of 8.4.1, using the relations ([CH] 4 (17)). The equality of the 1st and the 3rd members of (9.1.2) is none other than Lemma 3 of [1]. Paraphrasing the proof of (8.4.1), we see that
\[(9.1.2)\ \mathrm{bis}\qquad c_r(\check F)=\sum_{i=0}^r p^*(c_i(\check N))\xi^{r-i},\]
which completes the proof of (9.1.2). Applying the functor \(s^*\) to the two extreme members of (9.1.2), we see, since \(p\circ s=\mathrm{id}\), that (9.1.3) will be a consequence of the equality
\[s^*\left(\sum_{i=0}^{r-1}p^*(c_i(\check N))\xi^{r-i}\right)=0,\]
and a fortiori of the equalities
Let us prove (9.1.5). Denoting by \(\alpha:V(N)\longrightarrow\widehat N\) the canonical inclusion, we obviously have \(\alpha^*(\mathcal O_P(1))=\mathcal O_{V(N)}\), whence
\[\alpha^*(\xi)=\alpha^*(c_1(\mathcal O_P(1)))=c_1(\mathcal O_{V(N)})=0\]
by functoriality of Chern classes. The assertion follows at once by the definition of \(s\). Let us prove (9.1.4). For this we shall use the equality
\[(9.1.6)\qquad\xi\,c_r(\check F)=c_{r+1}\bigl(p^*(\check N)\oplus\mathcal O_P\bigr)=0,\]
which follows at once from the exact sequence (9.1.0). The element \(z\) admits a unique decomposition of the form
\[z=\sum_{i=0}^r p^*(a_i)\xi^i,\qquad\text{with }a_i\in C^*(S),\]
([CH] 4 Th 1). From (9.1.5) it follows that \(s^*(z)=a_0\). Moreover, the equality (9.1.6) shows that \(z\,c_r(\check F)=p^*(a_0)c_r(\check F)\), and we conclude by (9.1.1).
Let us now state the “self-intersection formula” in the Chow ring.

THEOREM 9.2 (MUMFORD).— Let \(k\) be a separably closed field and \(i:Y\hookrightarrow X\) a closed immersion, purely of codimension \(d\), of quasi-projective and smooth \(k\)-schemes. Then, denoting by \(N\) the normal sheaf of \(i\), we have for every \(y\in C^*(Y)\) the equality
\[i^*i_*(y)=y\,c_d(\check N)\]

in \(C^*(Y)\).
For the proof, we shall need a certain number of notations and auxiliary schemes.
Let us embed \(X\) in \(Z=X\times_k\mathbf P_k^1\) by means of the morphism \(u:t\mapsto(t,0)\), so that we have a cartesian diagram
\[ \begin{CD} \widehat N @>{j_1}>> Z'\\ @V{g_1}VV @VV{f_1}V\\ Y @>{i_1}>> Z, \end{CD} \]

where \(Z'\) denotes the blow-up of \(Z\) along \(Y\), and \(\widehat N=P_Y(N\oplus\mathcal O_Y)\) is the projective completion of the vector bundle \(V(N)\). As in the statement of 9.1, we denote by \(s:Y\hookrightarrow\widehat N\) the morphism composed of the zero section of \(V(N)\) and the canonical inclusion \(V(N)\hookrightarrow\widehat N\).

The inclusion \(Y\subset X\) allows us to identify \(Y\times\mathbf P^1=W\) with a closed subscheme of \(X\times\mathbf P^1\). We denote by \(W'\) the “pure transform of \(Y\times\mathbf P^1\) under \(f_1\)”, i.e. the subscheme of \(Z'\) which is the blow-up of \(Y\times\mathbf P^1\) along \(Y=Y\times0\). It is clear that \(W'\cap\widehat N\) is identified with the zero section of \(\widehat N\) and that, since \(Y\) is of codimension 1 in \(Y\times\mathbf P^1\), the canonical morphism \(f_2:W'\longrightarrow Y\times\mathbf P^1\) is an isomorphism. The other notations relative to \(W'\) are fixed by the canonical diagram

\[ \tag{$D_1$} \begin{CD} & \widehat N @>{j_1}>> Z'\\ @A{s}AA @A{\delta}AA @A{\beta}AA @VV{f_1}V\\ Y @>{\delta}>> W' @>{\beta}>> Z'\\ @V{\mathrm{id}}VV @V{f_2}_{\sim}VV\\ Y @>{\gamma}>> Y\times\mathbf P^1 @>{\alpha}>> X\times\mathbf P^1. \end{CD} \]

Similarly, denoting by \(X'\) the closed subscheme of \(Z'\) which is the blow-up of \(X=X\times0\) along \(Y\), it is clear that \(P(N)=X'\cap\widehat N\) is identified with the hyperplane at infinity \(H\) of \(\widehat N\). The other notations relative to \(X'\) are fixed by the canonical diagram
\[ \tag{$D_2$} \begin{CD} & \widehat N @>{j_1}>> Z'\\ @A{k}AA @A{\beta}AA @A{v}AA @VV{f_1}V\\ H @>{j}>> X'\\ @V{g}VV @V{g_1}VV @VV{f}V\\ Y @>{i}>> X @>{u}>> X\times\mathbf P^1=Z. \end{CD} \]
We shall now give, in the form of a lemma, a certain number of relations useful for what follows. We denote the locally free \(\mathcal O_{\widehat N}\)-Module defined by the canonical exact sequence
\[(9.3.0)\qquad0\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow g_1^*(N\oplus\mathcal O_Y)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{\widehat N}(1)\longrightarrow0,\]
and we set
\[(9.3.1)\qquad\bar\xi=c_1(\mathcal O_{\widehat N}(1)).\]

LEMMA 9.3.— With the preceding notations, we have the relations.
\[(9.3.2)\qquad j_1^*(\beta_*(1))=c_d(\check E),\qquad\text{in }C^*(\widehat N).\] \[(9.3.3)\qquad j_1^*(v_*(1))=\bar\xi,\qquad\text{in }C^*(\widehat N).\] \[(9.3.4)\qquad f_1^*u_*(1)=(j_1)_*(1)+v_*(1),\qquad\text{in }C^*(Z').\]

\[\left\{\begin{aligned}j_1^*\beta_*(1)&=s_*(1),\\j_1^*v_*(1)&=k_*(1).\end{aligned}\right.\]

The equalities (9.3.2) and (9.3.3) then come from (9.1.2) and from the normalization of Chern classes ([CH] 4-18) respectively. Let us prove (9.3.4). The cycle \(X'\) of \(Z'\) being the pure transform of \(X\times0\) under \(f_1\), we have a priori an equality of the form

LEMMA 9.4.— For every \(y\in C^*(\widehat N)\), we have:
\[c_d(\check E)j_1^*(j_1)_*(y)=0.\]
Proof: The element \(y\) admits in \(C^*(\widehat N)\) a unique decomposition of the form
\[y=\sum_{0\leq p\leq d}g_1^*(a_p)\bar\xi^p,\]
that is, by (9.3.3),

Let us finally show how the preceding implies the theorem. Let us denote by \(p:X\times\mathbf P_k^1\to X\) the first projection, so that \(i=p\circ i_1\). If \(y\in C^*(Y)\), we obviously have
\[(9.5.1)\qquad i^*i_*(y)=(i_1)^*(\bar x),\qquad\text{with }\bar x=p^*i_*(y).\]
By (9.1.1), we deduce that
\[i^*i_*(y)=(g_1)_*\bigl[g_1^*i_1^*(\bar x)c_d(\check E)\bigr],\]
that is

To prove the theorem, we may, by linearity, assume that \(y\) is the irreducible cycle associated with a subvariety \(T\) of \(Y\), so that \(\bar x\) corresponds to the cycle \(T\times\mathbf P^1\) of \(Z=X\times\mathbf P^1\). Denoting by \(\theta\) the pure transform under \(f_1\) of \(T\times\mathbf P^1\), i.e. the closure of the inverse image of \(T\times\mathbf P^1\) above \(Z-Y\), we have a relation of the form
\[(9.5.3)\qquad f_1^*(\bar x)=\theta+(j_1)_*(w),\qquad\text{with }w\in C^*(\widehat N).\]
Let us substitute this expression into (9.5.2). It then follows from 9.4 that
\[(9.5.4)\qquad i^*i_*(y)=(g_1)_*\bigl[j_1^*(\theta)c_d(\check E)\bigr],\]
whence, by (9.1.4),

But, since the (reduced) scheme underlying \(\theta\) is the blow-up of \(T\times\mathbf P^1\) along \(T=(T\times\mathbf P^1)\cap Y\), it cuts the zero section of \(\widehat N\) transversally along the image of \(T\) under the said zero section. Consequently,
which, taking into account (9.5.5) and (9.1.3), completes the proof of the theorem.

Let us now come to the "key formula" mentioned in the introduction. The notations adopted, for the most part recalled, are those of 4. It will be observed that they are compatible with those introduced in the present paragraph, except that the scheme denoted \(Y'\) in 4 is here denoted \(H\) (for "hyperplane"). We shall moreover use both notations concurrently.

PROPOSITION 9.6.— Let \(k\) be a separably closed field and \(i:Y\hookrightarrow X\) a closed immersion, purely of codimension \(d\), of quasi-projective and smooth \(k\)-schemes. Denoting by \(f:X'\to X\) the \(X\)-scheme obtained by blowing up \(X\) along \(Y\), let
\[\xymatrix{Y'\ar@{^{(}->}[r]^j\ar[d]_g&X'\ar[d]^f\\Y\ar@{^{(}->}[r]^i&X}\]

be the cartesian diagram constructed from \(f\) and \(i\). Then, denoting by \(F\) the locally free \(\mathcal O_{Y'}\)-Module defined by the canonical exact sequence

\[(9.6.1)\qquad0\to F\to g^*(N)\to\mathcal O_{Y'}(1)\to0,\]

we have the equality

\[(9.6.2)\qquad f^*i_*(y)=j_*\bigl(g^*(y)c_{d-1}(\check F)\bigr),\]

in \(C^*(X')\), for every \(y\in C^*(Y)\).
Before proving 9.6, we shall extract, in the form of a lemma, some properties of the diagrams \((D_1)\) and \((D_2)\) which have not been established up to now.

LEMMA 9.7.— We have the equalities:
\[(i)\qquad c_d(\check E)=k_*\bigl(c_{d-1}(\check F)\bigr)+(g_1)^*\bigl(c_d(\check N)\bigr).\] \[(ii)\qquad \beta_*f_2^*\gamma_*(y)=f_1^*\alpha_*\gamma_*(y)\quad\text{for all }y\in C^*(Y).\] \[(iii)\qquad f_1^*u_*(t)=(j_1)_*\bigl[(i\circ g_1)^*(t)\bigr]+v_*f^*(t)\quad\text{for all }t\in C^*(X).\]
Proof: Let us prove (i). We set \(\xi=c_1(\mathcal O_{Y'}(1))=k^*(\bar\xi)\). Paraphrasing the proof of (8.4.1), we obtain the equalities:
\[(9.7.1)\qquad c_d(\check E)=\sum_{p=0}^d g_1^*\bigl(c_p(\check N)\bigr)\bar\xi^{d-p}.\] \[(9.7.2)\qquad c_{d-1}(\check F)=\sum_{q=0}^{d-1}g^*\bigl(c_q(\check N)\bigr)\xi^{d-1-q}.\]

On the other hand, the structure of the Chow ring of a projective bundle, and in particular the relations
\[g_*(\xi^i)=0\quad\text{for }i<d-1\ ;\quad g_*(\xi^{d-1})=1\]
show that
\[\operatorname{Ker}(g_*)=g^*C^{p-1}(Y)\oplus g^*C^{p-2}(Y)\xi\oplus\cdots\oplus g^*C^{p-d+1}(Y)\xi^{d-2}.\]
In other words, \(\bar\mu\) can be interpreted as an isomorphism
\[(9.10.2)\qquad C^{p-1}(Y)\oplus C^{p-2}(Y)\oplus\cdots\oplus C^{p-d+1}(Y)\oplus C^p(X)\xrightarrow{\sim}C^p(X'),\]
whose inverse isomorphism is easy to exhibit.
As MURRE pointed out to me, the statement (9.10.2) is proved for \(d=2\) by SAMUEL (Relations d'équivalence, Proceedings of the International Congress, Edinburgh 1958).
Of course, in the setting of étale or \(\ell\)-adic cohomology we have a statement analogous to (9.10.2), which this time is deduced from (8.5).
